1.1 Motivation
Interrupt-driven programs are integral components of modern
safety-critical systems that require timely and reliable responses to
external events, such as automotive electronics, and medical equip-
ment [12, 28, 45, 50]. These programs leverage interrupt service
routines (ISRs) to handle asynchronous events, which introduces
non-deterministic instruction execution orders [48, 59]. As a result,
developers often struggle to avoid atomicity violation (AV) bugs,
which occur when the operation sequence on shared resources (e.g.,
variables) is disrupted by concurrent ISR execution [17, 19]. Such

2.1 [3].
violations undermine software safety and correctness, underscoring
the need for effective detection solutions [46, 52].
To illustrate the atomicity violation bugs, consider a simplified
example from the time calculation module of an embedded sys-
tem [3]. As shown in Figure 1, the global shared variable star_-
time.second is referenced in two places: an increment operation
in the main program and an assignment operation in the ISR. The
developer expects the increment operation to be atomic — the read
and write operations should execute consecutively (as shown by
green cycles). However, due to ISR preemption, the main program’s
execution may be interrupted, allowing the ISR to assign star_-
time.second a value of 1 before the increment operation completes
(as shown by red cycles). This results in an atomicity violation.
While seems straightforward, this type of defect turns out to
be hard to detect. Interrupts can be triggered at unpredictable
times,  leading to a huge state space in interrupt-driven pro-
grams [30, 39, 48]. It is computationally infeasible to exhaustively
explore all possible instruction execution orders to identify poten-
tial atomicity violations. Although only containing nine lines of
code, Figure 1 actually involves six states caused by different in-
terrupt points. Furthermore, shared resources are often referenced
through intricate mechanisms, including function call chains (e.g.,
nested_func) and conditional branches. This further complicates
the analysis of execution traces.
This example has demonstrated several open challenges in the
automated detection of atomicity violations in interrupt-driven
programs.

2.1  Interrupt-Driven Program
Interrupt-driven programs leverage interrupt mechanisms to man-
age concurrency in embedded systems, enabling real-time fea-
tures [29, 43]. An interrupt-driven program typically contains one
main program and several interrupt service routines (ISRs) [44].
When an interrupt occurs, the corresponding ISR may or may not
preempt the current execution flow, depending on its priority [49].
The communication between the main program and ISRs is realized
by global shared variables. Due to the non-deterministic nature
of interrupts, the execution order in interrupt-driven programs is
inherently unpredictable.
Each ISR is assigned a unique priority level. A high-priority ISR
can preempt the execution of a low-priority ISR. Once the high-
priority ISR is completed, the interrupted ISR resumes its execution.
The main program is designated the lowest priority to ensure
the preemption of all ISRs.
2.2  Atomicity Violation
In interrupt-driven programs, an atomicity violation occurs when
the intended operation sequence of a global shared variable is dis-
rupted by ISRs, violating the expected atomic order [21, 29]. An

3.1  Overview
Clover is a novel framework for detecting atomicity violations in
real-world interrupt-driven programs, integrating static analysis
and LLM agents. As illustrated in Figure 3, Clover consists of three
components: the code extractor, the expert agent, and the judge agent.
Given source code as input, the code extractor first performs static
analysis to identify shared resources between themain programand
ISRs, typically in the formof global shared variables. It then analyzes
the reference information of each shared resource, flagging the
critical ones that may suffer atomicity violations. For each critical
resource, this module extracts a core code snippet that contains
sufficient information for detecting atomicity violation.
The expert agent then invokes an LLM for code comprehension
and generates comments on the code snippet. Based on the reference
sequences of critical variables, it equips the LLM with knowledge
of different atomic violation patterns. It also provides the LLMwith

work of Clover.
source code, comments, and other information from earlier steps
to detect atomicity violation defects.
For all defects detected by the expert agent, the judge agent an-
alyzes the possible execution traces of the program to validate
whether the atomicity violation can actually be triggered, equipping
the LLM with interrupt-switch knowledge. It engages in multiple
rounds of communication with the expert agent until the defect re-
port is finalized or the maximum number of communication rounds
is reached.
The final defect report includes the atomicity violation pattern,
relevant code, interrupt details, and the maximum number of times
each defect can be triggered in one execution.
3.2  Code Extractor
Real-world interrupt-driven programs often contain hundreds of
global variables sharing across the main program and ISRs, while
only a subset of them susceptible to atomicity violations. For in-
stance, atomicity violations cannot occur on variables that are solely
referenced with read operations. Additionally, within the large code
base, only a small fraction is directly related to the interrupt mech-
anism and the references to global shared variables. Due to the
limitations of LLMs’ context windows and attention mechanisms,
directly applying them to analyze the entire code base is neither
feasible nor efficient.
Based on these insights, the code extractor aims to identify the
critical variables prone to atomicity violations and extracts a small
code snippet that contains all the relevant operations and interrupt
mechanisms. This module contains three major steps.
First, it conducts data flow analysis and function call analysis to
identify all global variables and their references. This static analysis
procedure is built on LLVM [2] and operates at the intermediate
representation (IR) level. For each reference, Clover records the op-
eration type (i.e., read or write), code location, source code, enclosed
function, and other interrupt-related information.
Next, Clover applies preliminary filtering on the global vari-
ables. A variable is considered critical only if its references follow
at least one atomicity violation pattern (see Figure 2). Specifically,
for a pattern < 𝑎1, 𝑎2, 𝑎3 > and a global variable 𝑥, Clover exam-
ines whether there exists a function containing consecutive 𝑎1-𝑎3
operations on 𝑥 and whether a higher-priority function contains
an 𝑎2 operation on 𝑥. If both conditions are satisfied, Clover flags
𝑥 for further examination to confirm the existence of < 𝑎1, 𝑎2, 𝑎3 >
defect.

Figure 4: The result of code extractor of the code in Figure 1.
Finally, for each critical variable 𝑥, Clover extracts a core code
snippet through function call analysis. It identifies all the functions
that references 𝑥 and traces their callers until reach the entry func-
tion of main program or an ISR. All these functions, together with
relevant top-level code, form the final code snippet for LLM agents
to analyze.

The interrupt priority of each ISR and related functions
are also extracted during this process.
Illustrative Example. Consider the code in Figure 1. The struct
variable star_time has two read and two write references, as
shown in Figure 4. The code extractor treats star_time as a single
entity, without distinguishing between its members (which is left
for later steps). Therefore, it flags two atomicity violation patterns
for further examination to confirm their existence.
3.3  Expert Agent
To enhance the capability of LLMs in identifying atomicity vi-
olations, the expert agent decomposes this task into two stages:
static-analysis-based code comprehension and LLM-based defect
detection with pattern guidance.
In the code comprehension stage, the expert agent leverages
static analysis results from the earlier step to annotate each code
statement that references the critical variable to be examined. These
annotations follow the template “[Read/Write] operation on [vari-
able]”, in the form of code comments. Particularly, the expert agent
only annotates the targeted critical variable, preventing LLM from
being distracted by other variables. Additionally, to assist LLMs,
which often struggle with identifying line numbers, this stage also
includes annotations that specify the line numbers where the oper-
ations occur.
In the defect detection stage, the expert agent guides the LLM
to analyze the semantics of critical variables, especially those in-
volving complex data structures (e.g., struct members). To facilitate
LLMs in understanding interrupt-driven programs, Clover con-
structs four knowledge modules, creating a pool of expert LLMs,
each responsible for detecting a specific type of atomicity violation
pattern. Each knowledge module provides a precise definition of the
corresponding pattern (see Figure 2), accompanied by a representa-
tive code example. During the analysis, the expert agent sequentially
invokes the relevant expert LLMs based on the patterns flagged by
the code extractor.

He et al.
$ Task Description
Detect  single-variable  atomicity  violations  involving  global
shared variables in Interrupt-Driven Programs. Focus exclusively  1
on  detecting  the  {Atomicity  Violation  Pattern}.  All  other
patterns are ignored.
$ Instruction
Step 1: Understand Program Structure.
- The interrupt-driven program consists of a main function and one
or more Interrupt Service Routines (ISRs).
- ISRs can preempt the main function or other ISRs.
- The main function executes sequentially, is not periodic, and
runs only once.
Step 2: Understand Interrupt Characteristics.
- Interrupt  Priority:  Higher  numbers  denote  higher  priority
interrupts.
- Interrupt Nesting: High-priority ISRs can preempt lower-priority
ISRs and the main function (Priority level: 0).  2
- Interrupt  Timing:  Interrupt  timing  is  unpredictable  and  may
occur at any point during the program execution.
Step 3: Identify Atomicity Violation Pattern.
{Domain Knowledge}
Step 4: Follow Key Rules for Detection.
- Track Global Shared Variables.
- Analyze Interrupt Priorities and Access Sequences.
- Decompose Compound Operations.
- Account for Interrupts in Loop Execution.
$ Output Format
After  thoroughly  checking  all  Read/Write  operations  on  global
shared variables, output a JSON report if a violation is detected:
```json
[Example Output]  3
```
If no violations are detected, output:
```plaintext
No defects.
```
$ Code Example
{Pattern Example}  4
$ INPUT
{Global variables and their read/write operations}

Priority  Low  High  Low
Figure 6: The result of Expert Agent of the code in Figure 1.
We carefully design the prompts for expert LLMs, incorporating
implicit chain-of-thought reasoning [58], as shown in Figure 5. To
enable the LLMs to handle variable references in diverse scenarios
and their interactions between ISRs, Clover explicitly requires
LLMs to analyze compound expressions (e.g., ++, and +=), loops,
and other control-flow structures, which native LLMs typically
struggle to comprehend. Specifically, the prompt consists of five
key components:
(1) Task description: Clearly specify the task to be performed and
the specific atomicity violation pattern to focus on.
(2)  Instruction: Provide step-by-step instructions, along with the
relevant domain knowledge module.
(3) Output format: Define the structure and content of defect report..

We have implemented Clover for C/C++ programs, the most popu-
lar programming languages for interrupt-driven software. The core
algorithm of Clover could be easily generalized to other program-
ming languages.
The code extractor employs Clang-6.0 [1] and LLVM-6.0 [2] to
perform static analysis. The expert and judge agents utilize Claude-
3.5-Sonnet [7] as their underlying LLM. To ensure deterministic
outputs, we set the model temperature to 0 and use greedy search
decoding [54]. By default, the multi-agent system executes a max-
imum of two iterations (𝑁 = 2) to refine the detection results. In

Our evaluation aims to answer three key research questions:
RQ1 (Effectiveness): How effective is Clover in detecting
atomicity violations in interrupt-driven programs, compared to
state-of-the-art approaches?
RQ2 (Scalability): How well does Clover scale when applied
to large-scale real-world programs?
RQ3 (Ablation): What are the individual contributions of
Clover’s core components to its overall performance?
5.1  Experimental Design and Settings
5.1.1  Benchmark. We evaluate Clover on both academic and in-
dustry benchmarks, as shown in Table 1.
• RaceBench 2.1 [3]: A widely used benchmark for atomicity
violations. It contains 31 interrupt-driven C programs with
handcrafted atomicity violation defects. It is designed to
simulate common atomicity violation patterns in real-world
programs.
• SV-COMP [4]: A software verification dataset that covers
various defect types. Following the evaluation settings of
CPA4AV [62], we adopt the ldv-races, goblint regression, and
pthread packages, transforming multi-threaded programs
into interrupt-driven ones while preserving their code logic
and structure.
• RWIP [5]: A collection of 18 real-world interrupt-driven
programs derived from embedded systems, including tem-
perature logging devices, MSP430 micro-controllers, brake-
by-wire systems, and i8xx chipsets.
5.1.2  Baselines. We compare Clover with three state-of-the-art
approaches, each leveraging distinct techniques for atomicity vio-
lation detection.
• intAtom [30]: It conducts pattern-based static analysis and
utilizes staged path pruning combined with constraint solv-
ing to efficiently reduce false positives.
• CPA4AV [62]: It is a static analysis tool, utilizing control-
flow graphs (CFGs) to identify interruption points where
ISRs may interfere with tasks with higher priorities.

intAtom  DRB-LLM
Figure 9: The correctly detected atomicity violations in three
benchmarks.
• DRB-LLM [11]: It is a recent purely LLM-based approach de-
signed for detecting data races in OpenMP programs. It uses
the DRB-ML dataset fromDataRaceBench [33] for in-context
learning (ICL). We configure it with the same LLM settings
as Clover, and strictly follow its original ICL methodology.
To the best of our knowledge, we are the first to adopt LLM
for atomicity violation detection. As DRB-LLM represents
the closest related work leveraging LLMs for similar defect
detection tasks, we include it as one of the baselines.
5.1.3  Metrics. An atomicity violation is considered successfully
detected when both its defect pattern and the sequence of three
operations (in the form of line numbers and operation types) are
correctly reported. Based on this criterion, we categorize the results
as follows:
• True Positive (TP): A correctly detected atomicity violation.
• False Positive (FP): An incorrectly reported atomicity violation.
• False Negative (FN): A ground-truth atomicity violation that is
not detected by an approach.
These values are subsequently utilized to compute the three key
evaluation metrics: precision, recall, and F1-score. Their average
values are computed collectively across all defects, rather than
averaging the metrics across benchmarks.
Notably, DRB-LLM is limited to detecting data races, which in-
volve only two operations rather than the three required for atom-
icity violations. Therefore, we adopt a more lenient evaluation
criterion for DRB-LLM, considering a prediction correct if it iden-
tifies a subset of the ground-truth operations, even if it does not
fully capture the defect pattern.
5.2  Answer to RQ1: Effectiveness
Table 2 presents a summary of Clover’s performance compared to
baselines across the three benchmarks with ground-truth. Overall,
Clover detects 86.6% of atomicity violations while maintaining a
low false positive rate of 7.7%. It achieves an average F1-score of
88.8%, significantly outperforming the baselines, which range from
40.7% to 69.7%.
In comparison, CPA4AV, the only baseline with comparable pre-
cision as Clover, fails to detect 39.2% more atomicity violations
than Clover. This limitation arises because CPA4AV struggles with
complex data structure, which is commonly found in real-world
programs (e.g., RWIP). In addition, it suffers program state space
explosion problem, preventing it from thoroughly analyzing the
program’s execution states. Similarly, intAtom cannot interpret the

worst performance.
Atomicity violation detection. Figure 9 summarizes the correctly
detected atomicity violations of each approach. Among them, 83
violations are not detected by any of the static analysis approaches
(i.e., intAtom and CP4AV) and 183 violations are missed by the
LLM-based approach (i.e., DRB-LLM). In contrast, Clover detects
the majority of them (331 out of 356), highlighting the advantage
of its hybrid methodology. Furthermore, Clover detects 63 defects
that none of the baselines is able to discover.
Time consumption. Due to network latency and the computation
cost of LLM inference in multi-agent process, Clover exhibits the
highest time consumption on small-scale programs.

However, as
program scale and complexity increases, the execution time of static
analysis approaches grows significantly. This trend is particularly
evident in the brake package (the largest one in RWIP), where the
execution time of CPA4AV surpasses that of Clover. Note that,
DRB-LLM is faster than Clover because it performs a simpler task
with a more straightforward design, as well as having less details
in the defect reports. However, its lower computational cost comes
at the expense of accuracy, achieving the lowest F1-score despite
being evaluated under a more lenient criterion.
Case Study. Table 3 details the performance of each approach on
RaceBench 2.1. Clover achieves the highest recall (92.9%) and F1-
score (88.9%). IntAtom performs competitively in this benchmark
as it is specifically designed for the RaceBench 2.1 and SV-COMP
benchmark. In contrast, CPA4AV, which supports only a subset of
code syntax, fails to execute on 17 programs. Meanwhile, DRB-LLM
struggles to understanding atomicity violations, producing false
positives in nearly all test cases.
Figure 10 illustrates an example containing three atomicity viola-
tion defects. Clover first identifies two critical resources, global_-
var and global_para, and extracts corresponding code snippets.
Through the multi-agent process, Clover accurately interprets the

control-flow structure and detects all three defects. In contrast, in-
tAtom detects only one defect related to global_para, failing to

Summary. Clover is highly effective in detecting atomicity
violates. It achieves a precision/recall of 92.3%/86.6%, outper-
forming existing approaches by 27.4-118.2% on F1-score.
5.3  Answer to RQ2: Scalability
To assess the scalability of Clover, we evaluate Clover on the
real-world interrupt-driven benchmarks: RWIP.
As summarized in Table 2, Clover successfully detects 78.6%
of atomicity violations while having a low false positive rate of
6.4%, demonstrating its effectiveness in analyzing large-scale real-
world programs. In contrast, DRB-LLM encounters context length
limitations, preventing it from processing several larger programs.
IntAtom fails to comprehend complex control flow structures and
extensive function call graphs, resulting in empty defect reports for
all programs. While CPA4AV achieves a precision comparable to
Clover, it only identifies 42.1% defects, as it struggles with complex
data structures and code logic.
Figure 11 illustrates a representative case containing four <
𝑅,𝑊, 𝑅 > atomicity violations related to the variable RT4_X0.
Clover identifies critical resources and extracts the core code,
allowing it to accurately and efficiently capture the interactions be-
tween the three ISRs operating on RT4_X0. Consequently, it detects
all four atomicity violations without any false positives. In contrast,
DRB-LLM fails to distinguish between the global variables with
similar identifiers (e.g., RT4_X0 and RT4_Buffer0). It fails to find
any defects, and even has one incorrect report. Due to the complex
control flow structure and huge function call graph, intAtom fails to
comprehend the program and does not report any defect. CPA4AV
terminates abnormally due to the state space explosion problem..

Internal threats to validity. Clover relies on static analysis to
extract critical code snippets, which may fail to capture operations
within third-party libraries or complex program dependencies. In
addition, LLMs inevitably make mistakes in detecting atomicity
violations, and which may not be eliminated through the limited
number of iterations of multi-agent conversation.
External threats to validity. Our evaluation is conducted on four
C/C++ benchmark suites, which may not represent all interrupt-
driven applications. The evaluation results may not generalize to
programs with different interrupt mechanisms, hardware platforms,
or software architectures. Clover is only evaluated on three LLM
settings. It may have a different performance from other LLMs.

7.1  Atomicity Violation Detection
Detecting atomicity violations in interrupt-driven software has
gained much attention in recent years, aiming to identify improper
operation sequences on shared resources when interrupts preempt
critical sections.
One line of work adopts static code analysis for this task. In-
tAtom [30] incorporates a Staged Path Pruning method, iteratively
discarding irrelevant execution paths in interrupt-driven programs.
CPA4AV [62] selects interrupt points and delaying ISR triggers,
in order to effectively reduce reliance on complex scheduling and
thus enhance detection accuracy. SpecChecker-ISA [53] leverages
abstract interpretation and numeric modeling to accurately locate
shared data access points in interrupt-driven software, thus reduc-
ing false positives.
Another line of work incorporates run-time information to en-
hance detection effectiveness. CTrigger [36] identifies feasible non-
serializable alternate executions by tracking and analyzing the pro-
gram. A-VIO [37] infers the programmer’s expectations regarding
